subjectLine: CD4+ T cells extend mouse lifespan 17 months via telomere Rivers 🧬
borisTake: The Sentcell preprint is either the most important paper published this decade or another spectacular mouse study that won't replicate — and I genuinely can't tell yet. What I do know is that T-cell immunosenescence is a legitimate target and the APC-to-T-cell telomere donation mechanism has prior peer-reviewed support; I'll be watching for independent cohort replication before adjusting any protocol.
leadAnalysis: CD4+ T cells appear to package surplus telomeres from antigen-presenting cells into extracellular structures the authors call 'Rivers,' which circulate systemically and can be adoptively transferred between animals to propagate rejuvenation independently of T cells. The mechanism builds directly on established immunological synapse biology — APC-to-T-cell telomere donation was peer-reviewed in Nature Cell Biology in 2022 — but the systemic, vessel-like distribution of these telomere structures and their cross-organism transplantability is entirely novel territory. In aged mice, direct River therapy extended median lifespan by ~17 months, with some animals surviving to nearly five years, which would be the largest life-extension result ever reported in a mammalian model. The extraordinary magnitude combined with small cohort sizes demands cautious interpretation, but the conceptual framework — transplantable rejuvenation via immune-mediated telomere recycling — is mechanistically coherent enough to justify close attention.
oneNumber: 17 | Months of median lifespan extension from CD4+ T-cell 'telomere River' therapy in aged mice (Sentcell preprint)

papers.title: CD4+ T cells confer transplantable rejuvenation via Rivers of telomeres
papers.summary: Young or metabolically reprogrammed CD4+ T cells produce extracellular telomere structures called 'Rivers' that circulate systemically and rejuvenate aged tissues. Adoptive transfer of Rivers alone extended median lifespan by ~17 months in aged mice. The pathway is conserved across kingdoms. Preprint — extraordinary claims, small cohorts, independent replication needed.

papers.title: Partial Reprogramming Is Conserved from Insect to Mammal
papers.summary: Yamanaka-factor-mediated partial reprogramming shows conserved epigenetic rejuvenation signatures from Drosophila through mice, strengthening the case that epigenetic age resetting is a fundamental biological mechanism rather than a mammal-specific artifact.

papers.title: Immunometabolic Resistors of Aging in Long-Lived Golden Spiny Mice
papers.summary: Golden spiny mice (Acomys russatus) raised under non-SPF conditions resist age-related frailty, retain high tissue repair capacity, and show reduced inflammaging and cellular senescence beyond 4 years. Macrophage metabolic rewiring appears central — pointing to innate immune programming as a tractable target for anti-aging intervention.

papers.title: Multidomain Lifestyle Intervention Improves Epigenetic Aging in Frail Older Adults: RCT
papers.summary: A randomised controlled trial shows that a combined exercise, nutrition, and cognitive training intervention measurably slows epigenetic clock progression in frail older adults. One of the cleaner demonstrations that lifestyle stacking moves biological age markers in humans.

papers.title: Pitavastatin Slows Epigenetic Aging in HIV: REPRIEVE Substudy
papers.summary: In the REPRIEVE cardiovascular trial, pitavastatin (a lipophilic statin) slowed epigenetic clock progression in HIV-positive adults on ART — a population with accelerated biological aging. Raises the possibility that statin-class drugs have off-target epigenetic benefits beyond lipid management.

papers.title: NAD+ Supplementation for Anti-Aging and Wellness: A PRISMA-Guided Systematic Review
papers.summary: Systematic review across human trials finds consistent improvements in energy metabolism, muscle function, and inflammatory markers with NMN/NR supplementation. Effect sizes remain modest in healthy adults; largest benefits seen in older or metabolically compromised populations. Does not settle the optimal precursor debate.

papers.title: Fenofibrate Delays Aging via the PPARα–CPT1C Axis
papers.summary: Fenofibrate, an existing fibrate drug, activates PPARα which upregulates CPT1C to enhance fatty acid oxidation and mitochondrial function — resulting in measurable lifespan extension in model organisms. Adds fibrate-class drugs to the growing list of existing pharmaceuticals with potential geroprotective mechanisms.
